title: Why Self-Management Is the Secret to Great Leadership (And Motherhood)
guestBio: Lynda Harvey is a former corporate leader at Verizon and Nordstrom turned viral TikTok educator and entrepreneur. She has built a thriving micro-learning platform focused on social leadership and is a mother of two teens.
excerpt: Former corporate executive Lynda Harvey reveals why the best leaders start by managing themselves first. From boardroom to living room, discover how self-awareness and emotional intelligence transform your leadership style.
metaDescription: Learn why self-management is the foundation of great leadership. Expert tips from corporate leader turned entrepreneur Lynda Harvey on leading yourself first.
keyTakeaways: Self-management is the prerequisite to effectively managing others in any leadership role.
Mothers naturally develop management skills through daily household coordination and family leadership.
Building a successful business requires transitioning from corporate thinking to entrepreneurial mindset.
Knowing who you don't want to be as a leader is equally important as defining who you want to become.
Tactical missions and small daily leadership moments build confidence for bigger leadership challenges.
Emotional intelligence and practical empathy are essential skills for modern leadership success.
Social media platforms like TikTok can be powerful tools for building educational business platforms.

pullQuotes.text: Leadership doesn't start in the boardroom — it starts in your own living room
Knowing who you don't want to be is just as important as knowing who you are

timestamps.label: Intro: Reframing Invisible Work as Leadership
Lynda's Corporate Journey: Verizon → Nordstrom → Entrepreneur
Knowing Who You Don't Want to Be

content:
What if everything you thought you knew about leadership was backwards? What if the path to managing others effectively doesn't start with learning management techniques, but with mastering the art of managing yourself first?

In this powerful conversation, Aimee sits down with Lynda Harvey, a former corporate powerhouse who climbed the ranks at Verizon and Nordstrom before pivoting to build her own thriving educational platform. Lynda's journey from corporate executive to viral TikTok educator offers a fresh perspective on what leadership really looks like in today's world.

The Hidden Leadership Skills You Already Have

Here's something that might surprise you: if you're a mother, you're already practicing advanced management skills every single day. Lynda breaks down how the invisible work of motherhood actually translates into powerful leadership capabilities that many women don't recognize or value in themselves.

Think about it. When you coordinate multiple schedules, mediate sibling conflicts, manage household logistics, and make countless daily decisions that keep your family functioning, you're exercising the same executive skills that corporate leaders get paid six figures to deploy. The difference? You've been doing it without a title, without recognition, and probably without giving yourself credit for the sophisticated management work you perform.

Lynda's insight challenges the traditional narrative that leadership skills must be learned in formal corporate environments. Instead, she argues that many women arrive at leadership roles already equipped with emotional intelligence, multitasking abilities, and the kind of practical empathy that makes teams thrive.

From Corporate Climber to Entrepreneur: The Mindset Shift

Lynda's transition from corporate executive to entrepreneur wasn't just a career change—it was a complete rewiring of how she thought about success, risk, and leadership. Her journey through major corporations like Verizon and Nordstrom gave her invaluable experience, but it also showed her the limitations of traditional corporate structures.

The leap to entrepreneurship required her to unlearn some of the corporate conditioning that had served her well as an employee but could hold her back as a business owner. She had to develop comfort with uncertainty, learn to make decisions without committees, and most importantly, trust her own judgment in ways that corporate life doesn't always encourage.

Her success on TikTok, where she built a following sharing leadership insights, demonstrates how modern entrepreneurship often requires embracing platforms and strategies that might seem unconventional to traditional business minds. But Lynda recognized that meeting people where they are—on social media, seeking bite-sized learning—was more important than adhering to conventional wisdom about professional platforms.

The Self-Management Foundation

The core message of this conversation centers on a simple but profound truth: you cannot effectively lead others until you learn to lead yourself. Self-management isn't just about personal productivity or organization—it's about developing the self-awareness, emotional regulation, and internal clarity that enables you to show up consistently for others.

Lynda explains that self-management involves understanding your triggers, knowing your values, recognizing your patterns, and developing systems that support your best self. When you master these internal dynamics, you create space to truly see and serve others rather than managing from a place of reactivity or overwhelm.

This is especially crucial for working mothers who often feel pulled in multiple directions. The temptation is to focus on managing all the external demands, but Lynda argues that starting with internal management—your energy, your boundaries, your responses—actually makes everything else more manageable.

Tactical Missions and Everyday Leadership

One of the most practical elements of Lynda's approach is her concept of tactical missions—small, specific leadership challenges that build confidence and skills incrementally. Rather than waiting for a formal leadership role to start developing leadership capabilities, she encourages women to look for everyday opportunities to practice leading.

These might be as simple as facilitating a difficult conversation, taking initiative on a project, or stepping up to solve a problem others are avoiding. Each of these moments is a chance to practice the skills you'll need in bigger leadership roles while also discovering more about your own leadership style and preferences.

This approach is particularly powerful for women who struggle with imposter syndrome or feel unprepared for leadership opportunities. By building a track record of small leadership wins, you develop both the competence and confidence needed for larger roles.

The Power of Negative Definition

Perhaps one of the most insightful parts of this conversation is Lynda's emphasis on defining yourself by who you don't want to be, not just who you do want to become. This reverse engineering approach to leadership development can be incredibly clarifying.

When you identify the leadership behaviors, communication styles, or management approaches that you find ineffective or harmful, you create clear boundaries for your own development. Maybe you've worked for a micromanager and know you want to lead with trust. Perhaps you've seen leaders who lack empathy and know that emotional intelligence will be central to your style.

This negative definition serves as both a compass and a guardrail, helping you make decisions that align with your values while avoiding the leadership pitfalls you've witnessed in others. It's a practical tool for anyone developing their leadership identity.

Leading in the New Era

Lynda's journey illustrates how leadership is evolving beyond traditional corporate hierarchies. Today's leaders need to be comfortable with ambiguity, skilled at building relationships across digital platforms, and able to create value through education and influence, not just authority.

Her success building an educational platform demonstrates that leadership now often means thought leadership—sharing insights, teaching others, and building communities around shared values and goals. This democratization of leadership creates opportunities for women to establish themselves as leaders without waiting for permission or formal appointments.

The conversation ultimately challenges you to examine your own relationship with leadership. Where might you already be leading without recognizing it? What self-management work do you need to do to show up more powerfully? And how can you start practicing leadership in small, everyday moments that build toward bigger opportunities?

Leadership isn't reserved for people with corner offices and impressive titles. It starts with managing yourself well enough to create space for others to thrive. And that work can begin today, right where you are, with exactly the experience and wisdom you already possess.
